items.html:
Consent runs through everyday care

Consent is not a form signed once and filed away. Every time you support someone — helping with a wash, offering a meal, assisting with medication — you need their agreement. Seeking consent shows respect and keeps your care lawful.

For consent to be valid it must be informed, given voluntarily, and given by someone with capacity for that particular decision. Informed means the person understands what you are proposing, why you are proposing it, and what the alternatives are, including the option of doing nothing at all.

Capacity is specific to the decision and the moment

The Mental Capacity Act treats capacity as decision-specific. Someone may have capacity to choose what to wear yet need support with a complex decision about their finances. You never judge capacity for everything at once.

Capacity is also time-specific. It can change from day to day, or even hour to hour — this is called fluctuating capacity.

  • Ask each time: a ‘yes’ yesterday is not consent for today.
  • Choose good moments: return when the person is more alert or comfortable.
  • Watch for change: illness, tiredness or medication can affect someone temporarily.

items.question: Mrs Okafor happily agreed to a shower yesterday, but this morning she is drowsy and turns away when you suggest it. What should you do?

answers.text: Treat her response now as the one that matters, offer again later when she is more alert, and record what happened.
answers.feedback: Correct. Consent must be sought each time; yesterday’s agreement does not carry over, and returning later respects her fluctuating capacity.

answers.text: Go ahead with the shower, because she consented to the same task yesterday.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Consent is time-specific; a previous ‘yes’ is not agreement for today.

answers.text: Decide she now lacks capacity for all decisions and make her choices for her.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Capacity is decision-specific, and one drowsy moment does not mean she lacks capacity for everything.

answers.text: Insist gently until she gives in, as personal care cannot wait.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Pressuring someone removes the voluntary nature of consent; a wash can usually wait a short while.

items.html:
Give every practicable help before deciding

The second principle of the Act is clear: you must not treat someone as unable to make a decision until you have taken all practicable steps to help them, without success. Lacking capacity is a conclusion you reach last, not an assumption you start with.

Many people can make their own choices when they are given the right support at the right time. Removing that support and then deciding a person cannot decide would be both unfair and unlawful.

Practical ways to support a decision

Small adjustments often make the difference between a decision made for someone and a decision made by them.

  • Communication: use plain language, pictures, objects or a communication aid, and involve a speech and language therapist where needed.
  • Timing: choose the time of day when the person is at their best, and never rush them.
  • Accessible information: break the choice into steps and present one piece at a time.
  • Environment: find a quiet, familiar place free of distractions.
  • People: include someone the person trusts if that is what they want.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the guidance on supporting people to make their own decisions.
items.textField: The second *principle* of the Act says you must take all *practicable* steps to help a person decide before treating them as unable. Using plain language and pictures supports their *communication*. Choosing the time of day when someone is most alert is about good *timing*. Deciding that a person lacks capacity should always be your *last* conclusion.

items.html:
People are allowed to make unwise decisions

The third principle of the Act protects a person’s right to make a decision that others think is unwise. A capable person can spend their savings freely, refuse a diet their dietitian recommends, or keep a home you feel is untidy. Disagreeing with a choice is not a reason to override it.

If we removed this right, care would quickly become about control rather than support, and people would lose the everyday freedoms the rest of us take for granted.

Never judge capacity by the outcome

It is tempting to decide that any risky or unusual choice must mean someone lacks capacity. The Act warns against exactly this — capacity is about how a person reaches a decision, not whether you approve of the result.

  • Look at the process: can the person understand, retain, weigh and communicate the relevant information?
  • Separate risk from capacity: a capable person may accept risks that you would not.
  • Notice real change: a sudden, out-of-character pattern of decisions may prompt a capacity assessment — a single unwise choice does not.

items.question: Mr Lang has capacity but keeps choosing foods his dietitian advised against. Because his choices are unwise, you can conclude he lacks capacity and decide his meals for him.
items.correctMsg: Correct — that would be judging capacity by the outcome. A person with capacity is entitled to make unwise choices; your role is to inform and support him, not to overrule him.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Making an unwise choice does not mean a person lacks capacity, and you must not judge capacity by whether you agree with the decision.

items.html:
How a DoLS authorisation works

Where a person in a care home or hospital lacks capacity to consent, and is kept under continuous supervision and not free to leave, the arrangement may amount to a deprivation of liberty. It must be authorised so that it stays lawful.

Two roles sit at the heart of the process. The managing authority is the care home or hospital providing the care; it must recognise when a deprivation may be happening and apply for authorisation. The supervisory body is the local authority, which arranges independent assessments and then grants or refuses the authorisation.

The move towards Liberty Protection Safeguards

DoLS only cover care homes and hospitals, and the process can be slow. To address this, the Mental Capacity (Amendment) Act 2019 created the Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS), designed to replace DoLS and to cover a wider range of settings, including supported living and a person’s own home.

  • Awareness only: implementation of LPS has been delayed, so DoLS remains the system currently in use.
  • Same purpose: both schemes exist to protect people who cannot consent to their care arrangements.
  • Your part is unchanged: notice when someone is not free to leave, and raise it.

items.question: In the DoLS process, which body applies for an authorisation, and which body grants it?

answers.text: The managing authority — the care home or hospital — applies, and the supervisory body, the local authority, grants it.
answers.feedback: Correct. The care home or hospital identifies the possible deprivation and applies; the local authority arranges assessments and decides.

answers.text: The local authority applies to the care home for permission to deprive the person of liberty.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. This reverses the roles; the care home or hospital applies to the local authority, not the other way round.

answers.text: The person’s family applies, and the GP grants the authorisation.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. DoLS is a formal legal process between the managing authority and the supervisory body, not between family and GP.

answers.text: The Court of Protection must authorise every deprivation in a care home.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. In care homes and hospitals DoLS is used; the Court of Protection is generally needed for other settings, not routine home or hospital cases.

items.html:
Good records protect everyone

The Mental Capacity Act runs on evidence. If a decision about someone’s care is ever questioned, your records show that you followed the principles. Vague notes leave the person unprotected and leave you exposed.

  • Record the support you gave: the steps you took to help the person make the decision themselves.
  • Note the specific decision: what was being decided, and when.
  • Show the reasoning: for a best-interests decision, who was consulted and what the person’s own wishes and feelings were.
  • Write promptly and factually: stick to what you actually saw and heard.

Putting it together: Grace’s tablets

Grace has early dementia. This morning she refuses her tablets, insisting she has ‘already had them’. You know she has not. Rather than pressing her or slipping the tablets into her tea, you presume capacity and try to help her decide: you explain calmly what the tablets are for and offer them again after breakfast.

Later, when she is more settled, Grace takes them willingly. You record what happened, the support you gave, and that her capacity for this decision appeared to fluctuate through the morning.

items.question: In the scenario, why was it right to offer Grace her tablets again after breakfast rather than hiding them in her tea?

answers.text: It respected her right to decide, allowed for her fluctuating capacity, and avoided covertly medicating her without lawful authority.
answers.feedback: Correct. Covert medication needs a capacity assessment and a formal best-interests process; presuming capacity and trying again is the right first step.

answers.text: Hiding tablets in food is quicker, so it was simply a matter of saving time.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Speed is not the issue; covert administration without proper authority would be unlawful.

answers.text: Because staff can never give medication to a person with dementia under any circumstances.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. People with dementia can and do take medication; the point is to seek consent and support the decision.

answers.text: Because once Grace refused, the tablets could not be offered again that day.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A refusal in one moment does not close the door; offering again later respects fluctuating capacity.
